## Methodology This map is generated through a six-phase pipeline that transforms Japanese government census and employment statistics into georeferenced origin/destination demand points. **Point Seeding:** Candidate points are placed within each small census area (町丁目) using population-weighted centroids derived from 100m building-footprint mesh data. Dense districts are split into multiple points using adaptive soft caps scaled by local density, while sparse rural areas use building footprint data to anchor points at actual settlement locations rather than geometric centers. **Mesh Reconciliation:** Initial point populations are refined by solving a constrained convex optimization that brings each point's mass closer to 250m residential mesh and 500m economic mesh ideals, while preserving the census totals for each 町丁目. Residents and workers are reconciled independently. **Aggregation:** A global scaling factor reconciles the mismatch between boundary-captured employed residents (from the National Census) and boundary-captured economic employees (from the Economic Census), since commuters cross the boundary in both directions. Small below-threshold points are merged into neighbors to maintain a minimum per-point mass. **Commute Assignment:** Commute flows are assigned using a gravity model with distance-decay, augmented by municipality-level origin-destination targets. An iterative calibration loop adjusts municipality-pair multipliers until simulated commute shares converge toward the observed inter-municipal worker flows. Destination capacity constraints prevent over-assignment. **Special Demand Injection:** Non-commuter demand generators — airports, ports, hospitals, schools, universities, and tourism attractions — are injected as an additive layer using data from MLIT, school surveys, and tourism statistics. Each facility's demand is estimated from its real-world throughput (e.g., annual passengers, bed occupancy, enrollment) and converted to a daily equivalent using facility-specific haircut factors. **Layout Reconciliation:** A final spatial pass resolves point overlaps and ensures map clickability by adjusting regular-point positions while keeping special-demand locations fixed. ## Data Sources All demand modeling in this map is derived from freely available Japanese government statistical datasets and public geospatial resources. - 令和2年国勢調査 (2020 National Census) — [e-Stat](https://www.e-stat.go.jp/) - 令和3年経済センサス (2021 Economic Census) — [e-Stat](https://www.e-stat.go.jp/) - 簡易100mメッシュ人口 (100m Mesh Population) — [GTFS-GIS](https://gtfs-gis.jp/teikyo/) - 国土数値情報 (National Land Numerical Information) — [MLIT](https://nlftp.mlit.go.jp/) - 空港管理状況調査 (Airport Management Statistics) — [MLIT Aviation](https://www.mlit.go.jp/koku/15_bf_000185.html) - 港湾調査 (Port Survey) — [MLIT Ports](https://www.mlit.go.jp/k-toukei/kouwan.html) - 大学ポートレート (University Portraits) — [JASSO](https://portraits.niad.ac.jp/) - 学校基本調査 (School Basic Survey) — [MEXT](https://www.mext.go.jp/) - 観光統計 (Tourism Statistics) — [JNTO](https://statistics.jnto.go.jp/) - Overture Maps Foundation — [Overture](https://overturemaps.org/) - 海しる / J-EGG500 (Ocean Information / Bathymetry) — [MSIL](https://www.msil.go.jp/) / [JODC](https://www.jodc.go.jp/) ## License This map data is released under the [MIT License](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT). ## Credits Map authored by [Yukina-](https://subwaybuildermodded.com/credits/)
